metadata.title: Homeostasis During Fasting. II. Hormone Substrate Differences Between Men and Women
metadata.journal: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Comparative fasting study with controlled intervention
metadata.demographics: Ten normal men and 9 normal women; Four men matched with 4 women in weight and age; Five women fasted in both preovulatory and postovulatory phases

evidence.quote: Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
The mean lowest glucose concentration in plasma in the male group during the 3 days of starvation was 66.4 ± 2.9 mg/100 ml. The corresponding figure in females was 47.8 ±2.9 mg/100 ml (p < 0.01).
Mean peak glucagon concentrations in women and men were, respectively, 230.0 ± 36.1 mig/ml and 121.8 ± 17.9 nng/ml (p < 0.01).
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.

methods_participants.label: Women fasted in menstrual cycle phases
methods_participants.value: 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding

evidence.quote: The former study was conducted 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbohydrate
Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.
Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.
FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg using palmitic acid standards and a 4 point standard curve
For glucagon measurements, samples were run in triplicate with appropriate control tubes for each sample.

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criterion - medication
methods_participants.value: No subject was receiving any type of medication

evidence.quote: No srarjject was receiving any type of medication.
Within 10% of ideal body weight according to New York Metropolitan Tables.

methods_participants.label: Glucose assay - coefficient of variation within assay

evidence.quote: Maximum variation in the basal range (0 to 15) was 1.0 u.U/ml.

methods_participants.label: Glucose assay - coefficient of variation between assays

narrative_results.statement: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight

evidence.quote: men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 describes menstrual cycle phases studied (preovulatory and postovulatory); pass_2 reports a count of 5 women fasted. These describe different information types and are not directly comparable
Pre-fasting duration (at least 2 weeks) vs control period duration (2 days) are different parameters with incompatible values
Control period duration (2 days) vs glucose assay method (glucose oxidase) are completely different parameters
Glucose measurement method vs FFA assay method are different parameters entirely
Glucose measurement replicates (triplicate) vs glucagon assay method are different parameters
FFA measurement method vs HGH assay method are different parameters entirely
FFA measurement replicates (duplicate) vs insulin assay method are different parameters
Pass 1 describes insulin measurement methodology (quadruplicate immunoassay), while Pass 2 describes participant demographics (4 men). These are fundamentally different types of information.
Pass_1 discusses plasma glucose levels in women vs men; Pass_2 discusses plasma glucagon concentrations. These are different metabolic markers and represent distinct findings.

Pass_1 reports glucagon concentrations higher in women; Pass_2 reports glucagon-glucose correlation coefficients and regression slopes. These are different analytical measures of different relationships.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The former study was conducted 4 to 5 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: 4 to 5 days after last day of menstrual bleeding
the latter, 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
Women fasted in menstrual cycle phases

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: 15 to 17 days after last day of menstrual bleeding
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbohydrate
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucose was measured in triplicate by a glucose oxidase method

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg using palmitic acid standards and a 4 point standard curve

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: To obtain maximum accuracy within the range of normal basal values of insulin, samples were run in quadruplicate, the final reported value being the mean of 4 determinations.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucagon was measured using a charcoal separatory method and antiserum for pancreatic glucagon (30 K) which was obtained from Dr. Roger Unger. For glucagon measurements, samples were run in triplicate with appropriate control tubes for each sample.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Serum immunoreactive insulin, HGH and glucagon were assayed by modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow, and Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson, respectively
Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson modification

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucose was measured in triplicate by a glucose oxidase method
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucose was measured in triplicate by a glucose oxidase method
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg using palmitic acid standards and a 4 point standard curve
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: FFA were measured in duplicate by the method of Trout, Estes and Friedberg
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbyhodrate and 1 g/kg of body weight of protein for at least 2 weeks.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Serum immunoreactive insulin, HGH and glucagon were assayed by modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow, and Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson, respectively
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: modification of Berson and Yalow method
To obtain maximum accuracy within the range of normal basal values of insulin, samples were run in quadruplicate, the final reported value being the mean of 4 determinations.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucagon was measured using a charcoal separatory method and antiserum for pancreatic glucagon (30 K)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: No srarjject was receiving any type of medication.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women during prolonged fasting, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change during fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma glucose decreased to a significantly lower level in women (52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr) compared to men (67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The correlation coefficient for glucagon and glucose for men was 0.64 (p < 0.01) and for women was 0.51 (p < .01). The slope of the regression line for men was —1.48 vs —2.20 for women.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men at 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The correlation coefficient for glucagon and glucose for men was 0.64 (p < 0.01) and for women was 0.51 (p < .01). The slope of the regression line for men was —1.48 vs —2.20 for women.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period (Fig. 2, Table 1).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in women fasted during both preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period in women fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phase.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Furthermore, men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: there is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: the data seems unequivocal in two separate respects : there is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting and probably, as a result, a greater increase of plasma FFA in women

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting and probably, as a result, a greater increase of plasma FFA in women.

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change during fasting
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: There was a striking sex-based difference in the pattern of glucose change. In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men. [sex difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucose decreased to a significantly lower level in women (52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr) compared to men (67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml) [lower in women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01). [sex difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men at 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast [higher in women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: The correlation coefficient for glucagon and glucose for men was 0.64 (p < 0.01) and for women was 0.51 (p < .01). The slope of the regression line for men was —1.48 vs —2.20 for women. [glucagon-glucose correlation]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in women fasted during both preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle [similar between phases]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Changes of plasma glucose and glucagon were similar in each study period in women fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phase. [menstrual cycle effect]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight [different from women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Men treated with ethinylestradiol did not exhibit a metabolic pattern comparable to women matched in age and weight. [estrogen effect]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting [greater decrease in women]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: There is a greater decrease of plasma glucose in women than in men during fasting and probably, as a result, a greater increase of plasma FFA in women. [sex difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women during prolonged fasting, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

semantic_groups.rationale: Both report 10 male participants; only terminology differs (Participant count vs Participant N)
Both report 9 female participants; only terminology differs (Participant count vs Participant N)
Both report 0.1 mg ethinylestradiol twice daily; pass_1 explicitly includes 'oral administration' while pass_2 omits it
Pass_1 describes menstrual cycle phases studied (preovulatory and postovulatory); pass_2 reports a count of 5 women fasted. These describe different information types and are not directly comparable
Pass_1 provides preovulatory phase timing (4-5 days after menstruation); pass_2 is empty/missing
Pass_1 provides postovulatory phase timing (15-17 days after menstruation); pass_2 is empty/missing
Both report minimum 150 g carbohydrate in pre-fasting diet; only phrasing differs
Both report 1 g/kg body weight protein in pre-fasting diet; only phrasing differs
Pre-fasting duration (at least 2 weeks) vs control period duration (2 days) are different parameters with incompatible values
Control period duration (2 days) vs glucose assay method (glucose oxidase) are completely different parameters
Both describe sampling frequency as twice daily (8 AM and 8 PM = twice daily)
Glucose measurement method vs FFA assay method are different parameters entirely
Glucose measurement replicates (triplicate) vs glucagon assay method are different parameters
FFA measurement method vs HGH assay method are different parameters entirely
FFA measurement replicates (duplicate) vs insulin assay method are different parameters
Pass 1 contains insulin measurement method information; pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 describes insulin measurement methodology (quadruplicate immunoassay), while Pass 2 describes participant demographics (4 men). These are fundamentally different types of information.
Pass 1 contains glucagon measurement method information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Pass 1 contains glucagon measurement replicates information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Pass 1 contains HGH measurement replicates information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Both describe the same inclusion criterion regarding carbohydrate tolerance. Pass 1 states no subject had intolerance, Pass 2 states normal - these convey the same meaning.
Both describe the same inclusion criterion regarding hepatic function. Pass 1 states screening tests were normal, Pass 2 states normal - same underlying fact.
Both describe the same inclusion criterion regarding medication use. Pass 1 states no medication received, Pass 2 states none - same underlying fact with minor wording difference.
Pass 1 contains body weight matching criteria information, but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Both describe the same fasting duration of 72 hours; minor formatting and label differences do not affect the underlying fact.
Pass 1 is empty while Pass 2 contains information about matched female participants (4). This is an orphan entry present only in Pass 2.
Both describe sex-based differences in glucose change pattern. Pass_2 provides specific numerical details while Pass_1 is more general, but they convey the same underlying fact.
Pass_1 discusses plasma glucose levels in women vs men; Pass_2 discusses plasma glucagon concentrations. These are different metabolic markers and represent distinct findings.

Pass_1 reports glucagon concentrations higher in women; Pass_2 reports glucagon-glucose correlation coefficients and regression slopes. These are different analytical measures of different relationships.

Both describe that glucose and glucagon changes were similar across menstrual cycle phases in women. Minor wording variation ('similar in women fasted during' vs 'similar in each study period in women fasted in') conveys the same finding.

Both state that men treated with ethinylestradiol did not show metabolic patterns comparable to age/weight-matched women. The difference in emphasis (estrogen effect vs different from women) is negligible.
Both describe greater plasma glucose decrease in women during fasting. Pass_2 adds information about FFA increase as a consequence, but the core finding is the same.

Both describe glucose and insulin concentration decreases in men and women during prolonged fasting, reaching plateau on day 2-3. Only minor wording differences ('With prolonged fasting' vs implied temporal sequence).
